title: Scaling Distributed Data with Consistent Hashing
description: Learn how to minimize data movement when scaling your database cluster. Map keys to nodes using a hash ring.

steps.detail: Drag the first Database node onto the canvas.

explanation.what: The primary storage for your sharded data.
explanation.why: When your data no longer fits on one server, you must partition it across many.
explanation.tradeoff: More servers means higher management complexity and potential for partial failure.
explanation.realWorld: DynamoDB uses this at its core to shard trillions of items across thousands of nodes.
explanation.capacity: Horizontal scaling: No theoretical limit on storage.

steps.detail: Drag another Database node to expand the cluster.

explanation.what: Expanding the cluster to increase throughput and storage capacity.
explanation.why: Consistent hashing ensures that when you add this node, only ~1/N of the data moves.
explanation.tradeoff: Resharding data while the system is under load can impact latency.
explanation.realWorld: Cassandra nodes join and leave the ring without downtime using gossip and consistent hashing.

steps.detail: Connect a Load Balancer to act as the hash ring manager.

explanation.what: Management layer that maps virtual positions on the hash ring to physical nodes.
explanation.why: Physical nodes aren't always identical. Virtual nodes (tokens) ensure a balanced distribution of data even when hardware is heterogeneous.
explanation.tradeoff: Higher metadata overhead for the hash ring mapping.
explanation.realWorld: Discord's message storage uses a ring with thousands of virtual nodes to prevent hot spots.

questions.question: If you have 4 nodes and add 1 more using 'mod n' hashing (not consistent), how much data needs to be moved?

explanation.correct: With simple 'mod n' hashing, the target node for almost every key changes when N increases. This is why consistent hashing is crucial.

wrongAnswers.a: 20% would be the ideal case (consistent hashing).
